diary: Sunday unfolded like watercolor—soft and unhurried. I woke to sunlight pooling across my kitchen, watched the lake shimmer while my coffee cooled. Bastille's cappuccino was perfect, as always. The waterfront at Gas Works was packed with boats and paddleboarders, all that energy and movement while I just stood there breathing it in. I grabbed ice cream in Georgetown almost as an afterthought, then drifted back home. Lunch by the window, then hours disappearing into sketching pottery designs and my transit map collection—there's something grounding about organizing those old maps, like holding onto pieces of the city. Whole Foods run felt necessary, purposeful. The salmon dinner was simple but felt special somehow, maybe because I took time with it. By evening, light was melting into the lake and I was just... content. Reading, sketching, watching the day wind down. One of those Sundays that asks nothing of you.
thought_process: The weather pulled me outside—I couldn't waste a 72-degree Sunday stuck indoors. I started with small rituals (coffee, pastry) that felt restorative after a work week, then let the day expand naturally toward Lake Union. Seeing all those boats and paddleboarders reminded me why I live here, but I didn't feel envious or restless; instead it felt like I was exactly where I needed to be, observing rather than participating. The ice cream was impulsive and silly, which felt right. Back home, I shifted into creative modes—sketching, organizing—because my hands needed something to do while my mind settled. Cooking became meditative rather than obligatory. The whole day had this permission built into it, no urgency, no external demands. I was responsive to my own rhythms instead of fighting them.
world_event_prompt: Calm, sunny Sunday, high of 72F. Quiet end-of-week day with no major disruptions. Lake Union sees heavy recreational boat and paddle traffic in the afternoon.
